passages.explanation: Paul arrives in Ephesus and finds some disciples. He asks them: did you receive the Holy Spirit when you believed? They reply: we haven't even heard that there is a Holy Spirit. He asks what baptism they received. John's baptism, they say. Paul explains that John's baptism was a baptism of repentance, pointing people to the one coming after him — Jesus. Hearing this, they're baptised in the name of the Lord Jesus. When Paul places his hands on them, the Holy Spirit comes on them, and they speak in tongues and prophesy. There are about twelve men in all. Like Apollos, these disciples have an incomplete picture. Paul doesn't dismiss what they have — he completes it.

Paul enters the synagogue and speaks boldly for three months, arguing persuasively about the kingdom of God. When some become obstinate and publicly malign the Way, Paul withdraws the disciples and begins daily discussions in the lecture hall of Tyrannus. This goes on for two years, so that all the Jews and Greeks in the province of Asia hear the word of the Lord. Paul essentially runs a teaching school. Ephesus becomes a hub, and the gospel radiates outward from his daily lectures — he doesn't need to travel to every city because people come to Ephesus from across the province.

God does extraordinary miracles through Paul — handkerchiefs and aprons that have touched him are taken to the sick, and their illnesses are cured and evil spirits leave. Some itinerant Jewish exorcists try to invoke the name of Jesus over people with evil spirits, saying: in the name of the Jesus whom Paul preaches, I command you. Seven sons of Sceva, a Jewish chief priest, are doing this. The evil spirit answers: Jesus I know, and Paul I know about, but who are you? The man with the evil spirit jumps on them, overpowers them all, and they flee naked and wounded. The name of Jesus isn't a magic formula — it requires a real relationship. The spirits know the difference.

News of the Sceva incident spreads through Ephesus. Jews and Greeks are seized with fear, and the name of the Lord Jesus is held in high honour. Many who believe now come and openly confess their evil practices. A number who had practiced sorcery bring their scrolls together and burn them publicly. The total value is calculated at fifty thousand silver coins — a fortune. Luke's summary: in this way the word of the Lord spread widely and grew in power. Real faith produces real change. Ephesus was famous for its magic arts, and the believers make a public break with their past.

Paul decides in the Spirit to go to Jerusalem by way of Macedonia and Achaia, saying: after I've been there, I must visit Rome also. He sends Timothy and Erastus ahead to Macedonia while he stays in Asia a little longer. This is the first mention of Paul's plan to reach Rome — the destination that will drive the rest of Acts. Paul's vision keeps expanding: Jerusalem, then the capital of the empire.

A silversmith named Demetrius, who makes silver shrines of Artemis and provides good business for the craftsmen, calls a meeting. He warns them: Paul has convinced large numbers of people that gods made by human hands are no gods at all. There's danger that their trade will lose its reputation and that the temple of the great goddess Artemis — one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World — will be discredited. The craftsmen are furious and begin shouting: great is Artemis of the Ephesians! The whole city is thrown into confusion. They rush to the theatre, dragging Paul's companions Gaius and Aristarchus. Paul wants to go before the crowd, but the disciples won't let him. Even some officials of the province who are friends of Paul send him a message urging him not to venture into the theatre. The assembly is in chaos — most people don't even know why they're there. The real issue is economic: the gospel is threatening a lucrative industry.

The city clerk quiets the crowd with a remarkably rational speech. He affirms Ephesus' status as guardian of Artemis' temple and her image that fell from heaven. Since this is undeniable, they should calm down. These men haven't robbed temples or blasphemed the goddess. If Demetrius has a legal complaint, courts are open and proconsuls are available — bring charges properly. If there's anything further, it can be settled in a legal assembly. As it stands, they risk being charged with rioting, since there's no justification for this commotion. With that, he dismisses the assembly. A bureaucrat saves the day. Roman authorities don't want unrest, and the clerk knows an unexplained riot could bring imperial investigation on the whole city.
